tateti.summaryMd: A two-player alignment game played on a 3 by 3 grid.
tateti.sourceLicenseLabel: CC BY-SA reference; rules text is original project wording
tateti.rulesMd:
## Objective
Be the first player to place three of your marks in one straight line: horizontal, vertical or diagonal.

## Materials
Use one sheet of paper and one pen or pencil. Draw a 3 by 3 grid. One player uses X and the other uses O.

## Setup
Choose the first player. The grid starts empty.

## Turn
On your turn, write your mark in one empty square. Players alternate turns and may not move or erase previous marks.

## End Of Game
A player wins immediately after completing three marks in a row, column or diagonal. If all nine squares are filled and no player has three in a line, the game is a draw.

paper.notes: One sheet is enough to draw the grid.

pen.notes: One pen or pencil is enough if players use X and O.

tutifruti.summaryMd: A word party game where players race to write category answers for a chosen letter.
tutifruti.sourceLabel: Traditional category word game; Scattergories reference
tutifruti.rulesMd:
## Objective
Score points by writing valid words that match several categories and start with the chosen letter.

## Materials
Each player needs paper and a pen. Use a timer, phone timer or agreed time limit.

## Setup
Agree on categories such as name, place, animal, object, food and color. Choose a random letter for the round.

## Round
Start the timer. Every player writes one answer per category using words that begin with the selected letter. Stop when time ends or when a player calls stop according to the house rule.

## Scoring
A valid answer scores if it fits the category and starts with the letter. A common scoring variant gives 10 points for a unique answer, 5 points for an answer shared with another player, and 0 points for blank or invalid answers.

## End Of Game
After the agreed number of rounds, the player with the highest total score wins.

paper.notes: Each player needs a score sheet or writing area.

pen.notes: Each player needs a pen or pencil.

timer.notes: Use any timer or phone timer for each round.

chess.summaryMd: A two-player abstract strategy game where checkmate is the winning condition.
chess.sourceLabel: FIDE Laws of Chess taking effect from 1 January 2023
chess.sourceLicenseLabel: External official reference; rules text is original project wording
chess.rulesMd:
## Objective
Chess is won by checkmating the opposing king. Checkmate means the king is under attack and the player to move has no legal move that removes the attack. A player may never make a move that leaves their own king in check, and the kings are never physically captured.

## Materials
Use one 8 by 8 chessboard and 32 pieces: for each side, one king, one queen, two rooks, two bishops, two knights and eight pawns. A chess clock and scoresheet are optional for casual play but standard in competition.

## Board Orientation
Place the board so each player has a light square on their near right corner. In algebraic notation, White's near-left square is a1 and White's near-right square is h1.

## Initial Setup
White starts on ranks 1 and 2; Black starts on ranks 8 and 7. Put rooks on the corners, knights next to the rooks, bishops next to the knights, the queen on her own color, and the king on the remaining central square. Pawns fill the rank directly in front of the pieces. White moves first.

## Turn Structure
Players alternate one legal move at a time. A move is legal only if the moving piece follows its movement rule, the destination is allowed, and the move does not leave that player's own king in check. A player may move to an empty square or capture an opposing piece on a square the piece can legally reach.

## Captures And Occupied Squares
A piece cannot move through another piece unless it is a knight. A piece cannot move to a square occupied by a friendly piece. When a piece captures, it moves to the opponent's occupied square and the captured piece is removed from the board.

## King
The king moves one square in any direction: horizontal, vertical or diagonal. The king may capture an opposing piece on an adjacent square if that square is not protected by the opponent. Kings may not move adjacent to each other because each king would attack the other.

## Queen
The queen moves any number of empty squares horizontally, vertically or diagonally. It combines rook and bishop movement. The queen cannot jump over pieces.

## Rook
A rook moves any number of empty squares horizontally or vertically. It cannot jump over pieces. Rooks are also used in castling if the castling requirements are met.

## Bishop
A bishop moves any number of empty squares diagonally. Each bishop remains on the same color of square for the whole game unless it is captured.

## Knight
A knight moves in an L shape: two squares in one straight direction and then one square perpendicular, or one square in one straight direction and then two perpendicular. Knights may jump over pieces between their starting and destination squares.

## Pawn Movement
A pawn normally moves one square forward toward the opponent's side of the board if that square is empty. From its starting square, a pawn may move two squares forward if both squares are empty. Pawns do not move backward.

## Pawn Captures
A pawn captures one square diagonally forward. It cannot capture straight ahead. If a pawn reaches the last rank, it must promote as part of that move.

## Promotion
When a pawn reaches the farthest rank from its starting side, it is replaced by a queen, rook, bishop or knight of the same color. The promoted piece is chosen by the player. Promotion is not limited to previously captured pieces, so a player may have more than one queen.

## En Passant
If a pawn moves two squares from its starting square and lands beside an opposing pawn, the opposing pawn may capture it as if it had moved only one square. This en passant capture must be made on the immediately following move or the right is lost.

## Castling
Castling is one king move involving the king and one rook. The king moves two squares toward the rook, and that rook moves to the square the king crossed. Kingside castling places the king on g1 or g8 and the rook on f1 or f8. Queenside castling places the king on c1 or c8 and the rook on d1 or d8.

## Castling Requirements
Castling is legal only if the king has not moved, the chosen rook has not moved, every square between them is empty, the king is not currently in check, the square the king crosses is not attacked, and the square the king lands on is not attacked. Castling cannot be used to escape through check or into check.

## Check
A king is in check when it is attacked by one or more opposing pieces. If a player is in check, their next move must remove the check by moving the king, capturing the attacking piece or blocking the attack. Knight checks and adjacent king attacks cannot be blocked.

## Checkmate
A checked player is checkmated if no legal move removes the check. The move that creates checkmate immediately ends the game and wins for the player who made it.

## Stalemate
If the player to move is not in check but has no legal move, the game is drawn by stalemate.

## Other Draws
A game is drawn if neither side can possibly checkmate by any legal sequence of moves, if both players agree to a draw after each side has made at least one move, or by applicable repeated-position and fifty-move rules in competitive play.

## Resignation
A player may resign. Resignation immediately ends the game and the opponent wins, unless the position is one where the opponent cannot possibly checkmate by any legal sequence, in which case the game is drawn under FIDE rules.

## Touch-Move For Formal Play
In formal chess, a player who deliberately touches one of their own pieces must move it if it has a legal move. A player who deliberately touches an opponent's piece must capture it if a legal capture is available. Casual players may agree not to enforce touch-move, but the rule should be known before play starts.

## Clock And Score For Formal Play
With a chess clock, a player completes a move by pressing their clock after making the move. In competitive games, illegal moves, flag fall, notation and conduct rules depend on the event regulations. For casual play, agree on whether clocks, takebacks and notation are being used before the first move.

chess-set.notes: One complete chess board and 32 pieces.

checkers.summaryMd: A two-player draughts game on an 8 by 8 board using diagonal movement and mandatory captures.
checkers.sourceLicenseLabel: External federation reference; rules text is original project wording
checkers.rulesMd:
## Objective
English draughts, also called American checkers or straight checkers, is won by capturing all opposing pieces or by blocking the opponent so they have no legal move.

## Materials
Use an 8 by 8 checkerboard and 24 checkers: 12 dark pieces for one player and 12 light pieces for the other.

## Board Orientation
Only the dark squares are used. Place the board so each player has a dark square at their near left corner and a light square at their near right corner.

## Setup
Each player places 12 pieces on the dark squares of the three rows nearest them. The two middle rows start empty. In standard English draughts, Black moves first unless the players agree otherwise.

## Men
Uncrowned pieces are called men. A man moves diagonally forward one square to an adjacent empty dark square. Men do not move backward except when a local variant says otherwise.

## Captures
If an opposing piece is diagonally forward adjacent to one of your men and the dark square immediately beyond it is empty, the man must jump over the opposing piece to that empty square and capture it. Captures are mandatory.

## Multiple Captures
After a capture, if the same piece can capture again, it must continue jumping in the same turn. Continue until that piece has no further capture. The player may choose among legal capture lines when more than one is available; English draughts does not require choosing the line that captures the most pieces.

## Crowning
When a man reaches the opponent's back row, it is crowned as a king. Usually a second checker is placed on top of it. In English draughts, if a man reaches the king row by a move or jump, the turn ends there and the new king may move as a king on a later turn.

## Kings
A king moves one square diagonally in any direction to an empty dark square. A king captures by jumping diagonally over an adjacent opposing piece to the empty dark square beyond it. Kings can move and capture forward or backward, but they are short kings: they do not fly across multiple squares.

## Illegal Moves
A player may not move to a light square, move onto an occupied square, move a man backward, skip a mandatory capture, or stop a multiple capture while another jump is available for the same piece.

## End Of Game
The game ends when one player has no pieces left, or when one player has pieces but no legal move. The other player wins. If neither player can realistically force progress, tournament rules or house rules may declare a draw after repeated positions or prolonged non-progress.

go.summaryMd: A two-player territory game where players place stones on intersections and surround territory.
go.sourceLicenseLabel: CC BY-SA reference; rules text is original project wording
go.rulesMd:
## Objective
Control more territory than the opponent after both players pass.

## Materials
Use a Go board, commonly 19 by 19 lines for the full game, and black and white stones.

## Setup
The board starts empty unless handicap stones are agreed. Black plays first.

## Turn
On your turn, place one stone of your color on an empty intersection or pass. Stones connected orthogonally form groups.

## Liberties And Captures
A group must have at least one empty adjacent intersection, called a liberty. When a move removes the last liberty from an opposing group, that group is captured and removed.

## Repetition And Suicide
Use the agreed ruleset for ko, superko and suicide. Beginner games should choose one ruleset before play starts.

## End Of Game
The game ends when both players pass consecutively. Score according to the chosen ruleset. Area scoring counts stones plus surrounded territory; territory scoring counts surrounded empty points and captures.

go-board.notes: One Go board; 19 by 19 is standard, smaller boards work for learning.

stones.notes: Black and white stones; use enough stones for the board size.

backgammon.summaryMd: A two-player race game where checkers move by dice rolls and bear off from the home board.
backgammon.sourceLicenseLabel: CC BY-SA reference; rules text is original project wording
backgammon.rulesMd:
## Objective
Move all of your checkers into your home board and then bear them off before your opponent does.

## Materials
Use a backgammon board, 15 checkers per player and two dice. A doubling cube is used for match or money play variants.

## Setup
Use the standard 24-point setup: each player starts with two checkers on their 24-point, five on their 13-point, three on their 8-point and five on their 6-point.

## Turn
Roll two dice and move checkers according to each die. A checker may move only to an open point: empty, occupied by your own checkers or occupied by exactly one opposing checker.

## Hitting And Entering
Landing on a point with one opposing checker hits it and sends it to the bar. A player with checkers on the bar must enter them into the opponent's home board before moving other checkers.

## Bearing Off
Once all your checkers are in your home board, you may bear them off using die rolls. The first player to bear off all checkers wins.

## Variants
Doubling, gammons and match scoring should be agreed before play.

dice.notes: Two dice are enough for casual play.

telestrations.summaryMd: A drawing-and-guessing party game playable with paper and pens using player-written prompts.
telestrations.sourceLabel: Telestrations public reference plus paper-and-pen adaptation
telestrations.rulesMd:
## Objective
Create a chain of prompts, drawings and guesses, then compare how the original prompt changed by the end of the round.

## Materials
Use paper and pens. Prompt cards are not required for this adaptation because players write their own starting prompts.

## Setup
Give each player a sheet or folded strip of paper. Each player secretly writes a short prompt at the top, then passes the paper to the next player.

## Round
The next player reads the prompt, folds or hides it, draws it, and passes the paper on. The following player looks only at the drawing, writes a guess, hides the drawing and passes it on. Continue alternating drawing and guessing until the paper returns to its owner or everyone has contributed.

## Reveal
Players read each chain aloud from the original prompt through the final guess.

## Scoring
For casual play, scoring is optional. If scoring is wanted, award points for funny favorite reveals, correct final guesses or best drawings by group vote.

## End Of Game
Play the agreed number of rounds. If scoring was used, the highest score wins; otherwise the reveal is the goal.

paper.notes: One sheet, notebook page or folded strip per player.

pen.notes: One pen or pencil per player.

pictionary.summaryMd: A team drawing game where players sketch clues while teammates race to guess the word or phrase.
pictionary.rulesMd:
## Objective
Be the first team to reach the agreed finish condition by correctly guessing words or phrases from drawings.

## Materials
Use paper, pens or pencils, a timer and prompt cards or a prepared list of prompts. If using a board edition, also use the board, pawns and category die included with that edition.

## Setup
Divide players into teams. Place paper where the active sketcher can draw clearly. Decide whether the group is playing with a board, a target score or a fixed number of rounds. Shuffle or prepare the prompt deck.

## Turn
On a team's turn, one player becomes the sketcher. Reveal a prompt only to the sketcher, start the timer and let the sketcher draw without speaking, writing letters or using numbers unless the group explicitly allows that variant. The sketcher's teammates may guess freely while the timer runs.

## Correct Guess
If the team guesses the prompt before time expires, they score the turn. In board play, advance according to the edition's movement rule. In score play, award one point. The next turn passes according to the table's chosen order.

## Failed Guess
If time expires before a correct guess, the team scores nothing and play passes to the next team.

## All-Play Variant
For an all-play prompt, each team chooses a sketcher and all teams draw at the same time. The first team to guess correctly wins that prompt.

## End Of Game
In board play, the first team to reach the final space and solve the required final prompt wins. In score play, the first team to reach the target score wins.

paper.notes: Use loose sheets, a notebook or a drawing pad.

pen.notes: Use one writing tool per active sketcher.

cards.notes: Use prompt cards, a prompt list or generated prompts.

timer.notes: Use the included timer or any phone timer.

codenames.summaryMd: A word-association team game where spymasters give one-word clues to identify hidden agents.
codenames.rulesMd:
## Objective
Your team wins by identifying all of its agents before the other team does, or immediately if the other team reveals the assassin.

## Materials
Use the codename word cards, key cards, agent tiles, bystander tiles, assassin tile and key card stand.

## Setup
Lay 25 codename cards in a 5 by 5 grid. Split players into two teams and choose one spymaster for each team. The spymasters sit where only they can see the key card. Draw one key card and place it in the stand. The key card assigns each grid position to the red team, blue team, neutral bystanders or the assassin. The starting team has one extra agent tile.

## Turn
The active spymaster gives exactly one clue word and one number. The clue should point to one or more cards belonging to that team. The number tells teammates how many cards the clue is intended to connect.

## Guessing
Teammates discuss and point to one card at a time. When a card is chosen, the spymaster covers it with the matching tile from the key. If it is their team's agent, they may continue guessing up to the clue number plus one optional extra guess. If it is a bystander or opposing agent, the turn ends. If it is the assassin, that team loses immediately.

## Clue Limits
A clue must relate to the meaning of the target words, not to spelling position, table position, card orientation or private information. Groups should agree before play how strict they want to be with proper nouns, abbreviations and homophones.

## End Of Game
A team wins when all of its agents are covered. A team loses immediately if it reveals the assassin.

cards.notes: Word cards, key cards and role tiles from a Codenames set.

just-one.summaryMd: A cooperative word game where players give one-word clues, but duplicate clues are cancelled.
just-one.rulesMd:
## Objective
Work together to guess as many mystery words as possible over the chosen set of cards.

## Materials
Use word cards, erasable boards or paper, markers or pencils and a way to hide each player's clue from the guesser.

## Setup
Shuffle the word cards and create the round deck. Choose one player as the first guesser. The guesser draws a card without looking at its words and chooses or is assigned one numbered word on the card.

## Clue Writing
All non-guessing players secretly write one clue word for the mystery word. A clue must be a single word. Do not use the mystery word itself, a direct translation, a same-family word or a clue that only changes spelling.

## Compare Clues
Before the guesser sees any clues, clue-givers reveal their words to each other. Any identical clues are cancelled and hidden from the guesser. Groups should also cancel clues that are effectively the same word by house agreement.

## Guess
The guesser sees only the remaining valid clues and makes one guess. The team scores a success if the guess matches the mystery word. If the guess is wrong, the team loses that card and usually also removes an additional card as penalty. The guesser may pass if the visible clues seem too risky.

## End Of Game
After the round deck is resolved, count successful guesses and compare the result to the group's scoring scale.

cards.notes: Use Just One word cards or a prepared list of mystery words.

boards.notes: Use erasable easels, small boards or paper slips for clues.

markers.notes: One marker or pencil per clue-giver.

werewolf.summaryMd: A social deduction game where villagers debate by day while werewolves secretly eliminate players at night.
werewolf.rulesMd:
## Objective
Werewolf is played by two hidden teams. The Villagers win when every Werewolf has been eliminated. The Werewolves win when the number of living Werewolves is equal to the number of living Villagers, because the village can no longer outvote them.

## Materials
Use one secret role card or handwritten role slip for every player. The recommended core roles are 1 Moderator, 1 Seer, 1 Doctor, 2 Werewolves and Villagers for everyone else. With more than 15 players, add another Werewolf for about every 4 additional players.

## Setup
Choose one person to be the Moderator. The Moderator does not play for either team and is responsible for dealing roles, running every night and day step, recording night choices and keeping a neutral tone.

Build the role deck for the player count, shuffle it and deal one face-down role to every player. Each player secretly checks their role and keeps it hidden. Players should sit in a circle if possible, because night actions are easier to run when everyone can point silently.

Before the first night, the Moderator announces the roles included in the game, the voting rule, whether eliminated players reveal their role, and whether the Doctor can protect themself. A clean beginner rule set is: eliminated players do not talk after death, majority vote is required to eliminate a player, and the Doctor may protect themself.

## First Night
The game starts at night. The Moderator says that night has fallen and tells everyone to close their eyes. Players may tap the table or their knees softly so accidental sounds do not reveal who is moving.

The Moderator wakes roles in the same order every night. Keeping the same order matters because changing it can leak information.

## Werewolf Turn
The Moderator says for the Werewolves to open their eyes. All Werewolves silently look around and identify each other. The Moderator notes who they are.

The Moderator then asks the Werewolves to choose a victim. The Werewolves must agree silently, usually by pointing at one living player. If they disagree, the Moderator waits until the group clearly points to the same target. The Moderator records the target and tells the Werewolves to close their eyes.

Werewolves should not speak during the night. Their job is to coordinate silently, avoid making identifying sounds, and later survive the daytime discussion.

## Doctor Turn
The Moderator asks the Doctor to open their eyes and choose one player to protect. The Doctor silently points to one living player. If the Werewolves chose that same player, the player survives the night. If the Doctor protects someone else, the Werewolf victim dies.

Use the agreed variant for self-protection and repeat protection. For beginner games, allowing the Doctor to protect themself keeps the rule simple. For stricter games, the group can forbid protecting the same player on two consecutive nights.

After the Moderator confirms the protected player, the Doctor closes their eyes.

## Seer Turn
The Moderator asks the Seer to open their eyes and choose one player to investigate. The Seer silently points to one living player. The Moderator silently answers whether that player is a Werewolf, commonly with thumbs up for Werewolf and thumbs down for not Werewolf.

The Seer must decide how to use that information during the day. Revealing too early can help the village, but it can also make the Seer an obvious target for the Werewolves.

After the Moderator answers, the Seer closes their eyes.

## Daybreak
The Moderator tells everyone to open their eyes and announces the night result. If the Werewolf victim was not protected, that player is eliminated immediately. If the Doctor saved the target, the Moderator announces that nobody died or that someone was saved, depending on the agreed style.

Eliminated players leave active play. They may no longer vote, give hints, react to accusations or reveal private information unless the group explicitly chose a variant that allows role reveal on death.

## Day Discussion
All living players discuss who they suspect. Villagers try to identify Werewolves from behavior, voting patterns and claims. Werewolves try to mislead the group, redirect suspicion and avoid being eliminated. The Seer may hint, claim or stay quiet. The Doctor may claim or hide, depending on what helps the village.

A practical discussion limit is 3 to 5 minutes. The Moderator should keep the conversation moving, stop dead players from influencing the game, and avoid facial expressions or timing that reveal secret information.

## Accusation And Defense
When a player wants to eliminate someone, they nominate that player. For a structured game, require a second player to support the nomination before it goes to a vote. The accused player then gets a short defense, usually about 30 seconds.

The group may allow one nomination at a time or several nominees before voting. Choose one method before the game starts.

## Voting
After discussion and defense, the Moderator calls for a vote. All living players vote publicly according to the chosen method, such as raised hands, pointing or thumbs. A majority vote eliminates the accused player.

If the vote does not reach a majority, the village continues discussion and may nominate again. If the group uses a day timer and no majority is reached before time expires, nobody is eliminated that day and night begins again.

For ties, use a known rule before play starts: either hold a runoff between tied players, reopen discussion briefly and vote again, or eliminate nobody.

## Nightfall And Later Rounds
After a player is eliminated by vote, or after the day ends without an elimination, night begins again. The Moderator repeats the same night order: Werewolves, Doctor, Seer, then daybreak.

If a role has been eliminated, the Moderator should still pause for that role or call the role in the same rhythm. This prevents the table from learning that a special role has died just because the night sequence became shorter.

## Winning
Check for victory after every night elimination and every daytime vote. The Villagers win immediately when no Werewolves remain. The Werewolves win immediately when the number of living Werewolves is equal to or greater than the number of living non-Werewolves.

If neither condition is true, continue alternating night and day rounds.

cards.notes: Use a Werewolf role deck or one handwritten role slip per player.

paper.notes: Useful for the Moderator to track roles, night targets and votes.

the-resistance.summaryMd: A hidden-role mission game where Resistance players approve teams while spies try to sabotage missions.
the-resistance.sourceLabel: Indie Boards and Cards: The Resistance
the-resistance.rulesMd:
## Objective
The Resistance wins by completing three successful missions. The spies win by causing three failed missions or by making the group reject five mission teams for the same mission.

## Materials
Use character cards for secret alignment, mission cards, vote cards, team cards or tokens, score markers, a round marker and a leader marker.

## Setup
Choose the player count and assign the correct number of Resistance members and spies for that count. Shuffle and deal one secret character card to each player. Pick a starting leader. The moderator or leader runs the spy reveal: all players close their eyes, spies open their eyes to identify each other, then close their eyes before everyone wakes.

## Mission Team Proposal
Each mission has a required team size based on player count and mission number. The leader proposes that many players for the mission. Players may discuss before and after the proposal.

## Team Vote
All players secretly choose approve or reject, then reveal votes together. If approvals are a strict majority, the mission team is accepted. If the vote ties or rejects are the majority, the team is rejected, leadership passes and the same mission is proposed again. If five teams are rejected for one mission, the spies win.

## Mission Resolution
Each player on the approved mission secretly submits a success or fail card. Resistance players must submit success. Spies may submit success or fail. Shuffle and reveal mission cards. The mission succeeds if no fail cards are revealed, except in variants or player counts where a mission may require more than one fail.

## End Of Game
Advance the score after each mission. The Resistance wins when three missions succeed. The spies win when three missions fail or when five team proposals fail on a single mission.

cards.notes: Character, vote and mission cards from The Resistance or equivalent role/vote slips.

tokens.notes: Use team markers, score markers and a leader marker.
